candidates.name: Ignorantia Juris Non Excusat
candidates.description: The principle that not knowing a rule does not exempt you from its consequences. Applied far beyond law: corporate compliance, social norms, professional standards, parenting. Encodes the structural insight that rule-systems work only if ignorance is not a valid defense -- otherwise every rule becomes optional for anyone willing to claim ignorance.

candidates.name: Caveat Emptor
candidates.description: Buyer bears the risk of a bad purchase. Migrated into universal advice about due diligence -- hiring decisions, technology adoption, information consumption. The metaphor frames every transaction (not just commercial) as carrying asymmetric information risk that the acquiring party must manage.

candidates.name: Res Ipsa Loquitur
candidates.description: When the outcome is so clearly the result of negligence that no further explanation is needed. Used in everyday reasoning about obvious causation: 'the code speaks for itself,' 'the numbers speak for themselves.' Encodes the principle that some evidence is self-explanatory and demanding further proof is obstruction.

candidates.name: Nemo Judex in Causa Sua
candidates.latin: Nemo debet esse judex in propria sua causa
candidates.description: No one should be judge in their own case. A foundational principle of fairness that migrated into organizational design (audit committees, peer review, separation of powers), scientific methodology (double-blind studies), and everyday ethics. The structural insight: self-assessment is inherently unreliable because interest corrupts judgment.

candidates.name: Audi Alteram Partem
candidates.description: Hear the other side before deciding. One of natural justice's two pillars. Applied everywhere: management, journalism, relationships, software code review. The metaphor frames decision-making as inherently adversarial -- every question has at least two legitimate perspectives, and hearing only one produces systematically biased outcomes.

candidates.name: De Minimis Non Curat Lex
candidates.description: The law ignores trivial matters. A resource-allocation principle that migrated into engineering (de minimis thresholds), management (don't sweat the small stuff), and systems design. Encodes the insight that any system with finite capacity must establish a materiality threshold below which it refuses to act, or else collapse under the weight of trivia.

candidates.name: Ubi Jus Ibi Remedium
candidates.description: Where a right exists, a remedy must exist to enforce it. A rights-without-remedies-are-meaningless principle. Applied to organizational accountability, software error handling, and political theory. The structural insight: declaring a right without providing enforcement is performative, not substantive.

candidates.name: Necessitas Non Habet Legem
candidates.description: Necessity overrides law. The principle that extreme circumstances suspend normal rules. Used to justify emergency powers, ethical boundary-crossing, and pragmatic exceptions. Encodes a tension at the heart of all rule-systems: rigid rules that cannot bend under extreme pressure will break catastrophically.

candidates.name: Nemo Dat Quod Non Habet
candidates.description: You cannot transfer a right you do not possess. Applied metaphorically to teaching (you cannot teach what you do not know), leadership (you cannot give what you lack), and delegation (authority cannot be delegated beyond what was granted). The structural constraint: transfer is bounded by possession.

candidates.name: Qui Facit Per Alium Facit Per Se
candidates.latin: Qui facit per alium facit per se
candidates.description: Acting through an agent is acting yourself. The foundation of vicarious liability and respondeat superior. Applied to management accountability, AI ethics (who is responsible for the algorithm's decision?), and organizational design. The metaphor collapses the principal-agent distinction: delegation does not dilute responsibility.

candidates.name: Respondeat Superior
candidates.description: The master must answer for the servant's acts. Applied beyond employment law to any hierarchical accountability structure: military command responsibility, corporate governance, parental liability. Encodes the principle that power and responsibility are structurally coupled -- you cannot exercise authority without accepting liability for its consequences.

candidates.name: Clean Hands Doctrine
candidates.latin: He who comes into equity must come with clean hands
candidates.description: You cannot seek justice while engaging in related injustice. The purity metaphor is doing heavy structural work: hands are clean or dirty, and dirtiness contaminates the moral standing of a claim. Applied to hypocrisy arguments in politics, credibility in debate, and organizational ethics. The metaphor frames moral standing as a substance that can be stained.

candidates.name: Fruit of the Poisonous Tree
candidates.latin: N/A (English doctrine, coined by Justice Frankfurter 1939)
candidates.description: Evidence obtained through illegal means is tainted and inadmissible, as is any evidence derived from it. The botanical metaphor encodes causal contamination: corruption at the root propagates through the entire chain. Applied to data provenance, supply chain ethics, and any reasoning about whether downstream results inherit the moral status of upstream methods.

candidates.name: Letter vs. Spirit of the Law
candidates.latin: Equity regards the spirit and not the letter
candidates.description: The tension between literal compliance and intended purpose. One of the most productive legal metaphors in everyday discourse: following the letter but violating the spirit of a contract, policy, or agreement. Encodes the fundamental hermeneutic problem: all written rules are underspecified, and rigid literalism enables gaming.

candidates.name: Hard Cases Make Bad Law
candidates.description: Extreme cases are poor foundations for general rules. Applied to policy design, software architecture, and organizational process. The structural insight: emotionally compelling edge cases distort judgment about what rules should govern the typical case. Exception-driven policy is systematically worse than baseline-driven policy.

candidates.name: Possession Is Nine-Tenths of the Law
candidates.description: Physical control creates a presumption of ownership. So deeply embedded in everyday reasoning that it functions as a dead metaphor -- people invoke it without thinking about its legal origins. Applied to information (whoever holds the data controls the narrative), territory, and organizational resources.

candidates.name: Nullus Commodum Capere Potest de Injuria Sua Propria
candidates.latin: Nullus commodum capere potest de injuria sua propria
candidates.description: No one should benefit from their own wrongdoing. Applied to insurance fraud prevention, corporate governance, inheritance disputes, and everyday fairness reasoning. The structural principle: systems must be designed so that bad actors cannot convert rule-breaking into advantage, or the rules become self-defeating.

candidates.name: Qui Tacet Consentire Videtur
candidates.description: Silence implies consent. A deeply influential legal maxim that migrated into social and political reasoning: 'silence is complicity,' 'if you see something, say something.' The metaphor frames inaction as a positive choice with moral weight, not a neutral default. Controversial because it shifts the burden from actors to bystanders.

candidates.name: Volenti Non Fit Injuria
candidates.description: A willing person cannot claim to have been wronged. The consent principle underlying contracts, sports, medical procedures, and risk-taking. Applied metaphorically: if you knew the risks and proceeded anyway, you accepted the consequences. Encodes the structural boundary between harm-done-to-you and harm-you-chose.

candidates.name: Dura Lex Sed Lex
candidates.description: The law may be harsh, but it is still the law. Encodes the tension between justice and legality, between what is right and what is rule-following. Applied to bureaucratic rigidity, organizational policy, and any situation where rules produce unjust outcomes but must still be obeyed. The structural insight: rule-of-law requires accepting bad outcomes from good rules.

candidates.name: Lex Non Cogit ad Impossibilia
candidates.description: The law does not compel the impossible. Applied to management expectations, contractual obligations, and performance standards. The structural principle: legitimate authority is bounded by feasibility. Demanding the impossible is not high standards -- it is structural bad faith that delegitimizes the authority making the demand.

candidates.name: Fiat Justitia Ruat Caelum
candidates.description: Justice must be done regardless of consequences. The absolutist counterpoint to consequentialist reasoning. Applied to whistleblowing, ethical stands, and principled resistance. Encodes a deontological commitment: some principles are non-negotiable regardless of cost. The metaphor's power lies in the cosmic scale of the concession -- even if the sky falls.

candidates.name: Falsus in Uno Falsus in Omnibus
candidates.latin: Falsus in uno falsus in omnibus
candidates.description: False in one thing, false in everything. A credibility heuristic: if someone lies about one thing, their entire testimony is suspect. Applied to brand trust, personal credibility, and data quality. Encodes a totalizing inference pattern that is powerful but dangerous -- it conflates local unreliability with global unreliability.

candidates.name: Sic Utere Tuo Ut Alienum Non Laedas
candidates.latin: Sic utere tuo ut alienum non laedas
candidates.description: Use your own property so as not to injure others. The foundational nuisance principle. Applied to noise ordinances, environmental regulation, internet governance, and any context where one party's exercise of rights imposes externalities on others. Encodes the structural insight that rights are not absolute -- they are bounded by their impact on others' rights.

candidates.name: Exceptio Probat Regulam
candidates.description: The exception proves (tests) the rule. One of the most widely misunderstood legal maxims. In its original sense, 'proves' means 'tests' -- an exception tests whether a rule is robust. In popular usage it has been inverted to mean that exceptions confirm rules. This semantic drift is itself interesting: a maxim about testing rules became a maxim about defending them.
